content:
Welcome to Lesson 2 of the ICT Mentorship June 2017 content: ICT Stock Trading
- Building Buy
Watchlists.

section_title: SCOPE: FOCUS ON DOW JONES INDUSTRIAL AVERAGE
content:
Our focus will be on the Dow Jones Industrial Composite stock list, which includes all 30 stocks currently
making up the Dow Jones Industrial Average.
- While these applications can be done on the S&P 500 and NASDAQ 100, it is not necessary to diversify,
as all required stock trades can be made from the Dow Jones 30 stock list alone.

section_title: SEASONAL INFLUENCES AND BULLISH MONTHS FOR STOCK TRADING
content:
As discussed in the first lesson, we will utilize seasonal influences per calendar month.
- The primary focus for building buy watchlists will be on bullish months.
- We are not looking for individual monthly trades, but rather for periods when the market generally wants
to trade higher.
- We will focus on two main segments of the calendar year for anticipating bullishness:
- The first half of the year
- The second half of the year

To build effective buy watchlists, apply the following filters:
- Filter 1: Is the stock market poised to rally? Is it technically sound for anticipating a market rally?
- Filter 2: During bullish months, select 'higher low stocks'
- those that have formed a higher low.
- Ideal long swing setups are typically found in:
- February to May months
- October to January months
- While previous tutorials focused on the fall (October to January), this lesson will emphasize the spring
session (February to May) and introduce additional insights.

section_title: GENERAL STOCK BEHAVIOR AND SELECTION TIPS
content:
Generally, the majority of index stocks tend to rise when the overall market or a major market rallies,
following the analogy: 'in high tide all boats rise'.
- Look for stocks that are trending higher on the weekly chart prior to the desired buying condition.
- Avoid 'safe' stocks like Verizon, General Electric (GE), or Coca-Cola.
- Strong stocks will display an obvious bullish structure.

section_title: IDENTIFYING ACCUMULATION AND LEADERSHIP STOCKS VIA SMT
content:
Discount arrays combined with Index SMT (Smart Money Technique) can identify companies undergoing
heavy accumulation during seasonally bullish months.
- Aim to narrow your selection to two to four companies during the stock selection process.
- Leadership stocks, those aggressively bought by institutions, will fail to drop lower during bullish months
when the three major stock indices (NASDAQ, S&P, and Dow) decline.
- This divergence, where one of those indices fails to post a lower low comparably, signals an overall
market trend change to bullishness.
- At this same juncture, we look for stocks that are making higher highs; if a particular stock does not
exhibit this, it should be discarded.

section_title: FOCUS ON FEBRUARY TO MAY SEASONAL BULLISHNESS
content:
Our current analysis focuses primarily on the February to May portion of the year for identifying bullish
stock opportunities.
- To save time, the Dow 30 stocks have been pre-screened based on the outlined criteria.

- RELATIVE STRENGTH EXAMPLE
The Dow Jones (solid green line) traded lower from January into mid-January 2017, making a lower low.
- Apple Incorporated (AAPL) failed to go lower at that same time, demonstrating relative strength.
- This scenario typically occurs when large institutions initiate buy programs, indicating significant
accumulation around the $118-$120 per share level.
- From February all the way up into May, Apple experienced a substantial appreciation.
- This 'failure to make a lower low' against the Dow Jones Industrial Average is a key hallmark of
institutional accumulation during bullish seasonal periods, leading to subsequent price appreciation.

- BULLISH DIVERGENCE AND DISCOUNT ARRAY
Boeing presented a similar scenario.
- In January, leading into February's bullish seasonal period, the Dow Jones created a lower low, but
Boeing's share price was unwilling to go lower, exhibiting a bullish divergence.
- Boeing gapped up in January, and that gap (a fair value gap or discount array) was closed on the second
trading day of February.
- Price then rallied away, moving higher into May.
- The bulk of the bullishness was observed between February and May, aligning with seasonal tendencies
for stock trades in the first portion of the year.

- HIGHER LOW AND SEASONAL SHIFT
Disney also met the criteria of forming a higher low, bucking the trend of a lower low in the Dow Jones
Industrial Average in January.
- Between February and May, Disney showed a nice appreciation in share price.
- However, in May, when seasonal tendencies shifted from bullishness, Disney made its high and
subsequently traded off rather precipitously on the downside.

- DIVERGENCE AND LIQUIDITY VOID FILL
Home Depot met the criteria.
- In January, the Dow Jones made a lower low, but Home Depot shares failed to make that lower low,
diverging around the $135-$136 per share price.
- A large up day in January created a liquidity void, which price later traded down into and closed in
February (closing that range fair value gap, a discount array).
- This led to a very nice price movement higher between the anticipated bullishness beginning in February
and extending into May.

- BULLISH DIVERGENCE AND AGGRESSIVE RUN
McDonald's also aligned with the criteria.
- The Dow Jones exhibited a lower low in January 2017, but McDonald's shares diverged bullishly by not
making a lower low in January.
- Price rallied higher, showing a very nice movement from February's inception of the bullish seasonal
tendency up to a share price of $142, and subsequently moved as high as $153 a share.

- UNWILLINGNESS TO MAKE LOWER LOW
Visa was also filtered through the process.
- The Dow Jones made a lower low in January, but Visa's share price was unwilling to make that lower low.
- Between February and May, a nice appreciation was observed from February's beginning to the start of
May.

Out of the 30 Dow stocks, after filtering out less exciting companies like Verizon, Coca-Cola, General
Electric, and personally, Microsoft and Intel, six stocks were identified:
- Apple (AAPL)
- Boeing (BA)
- Disney (DIS)
- Home Depot (HD)
- McDonald's (MCD)
- Visa (V)
- The next step is to select two to four from this list.

Further refinement of the watchlist involves considering:
- The affordability of call options.
- The historical trading context of each stock.
- Eliminating any stock that is too extended from a weekly or daily market structure.

section_title: LOW RESISTANCE LIQUIDITY RUNS AND INSTITUTIONAL PREFERENCES
content:
When we look for low resistance liquidity runs, we are anticipating a move through an old high or low,
representing a qualified break in market structure.
- For stocks moving higher, institutions prefer big breakouts and aggressive movements, especially evident
on a weekly chart.
- This is particularly true when fundamental analysis suggests higher prices for the company, the major
market is expected to move higher, and seasonal tendencies align.
- If a market structure high is already significantly higher than your potential entry point, you are likely to
experience lethargic price action, or the price may peter out and either go sideways or reverse altogether.

section_title: COMPREHENSIVE BLENDED TRADING PROCESS FOR STOCK SELECTION
content:
Quarterly increases in sales and profits are a strong fundamental factor for keeping a stock on the list.
- The ultimate goal is a blended trading process that combines simple seasonal, fundamental, and
technical analysis for selecting potentially winning stocks.
- We effectively narrowed down 30 initial stocks to six strong candidates.

- BREAKOUT POTENTIAL
Reviewing Apple's weekly chart:
- Apple had an old high back in 2015.
- At the time of the seasonal bullish tendency for February 2017, price was positioned to break out.
- This presented a low resistance liquidity run above $135, signaling that institutions would likely step in to
facilitate this breakout.
- Apple displayed a very strong weekly structure, with every down-close candle supporting new buying from
April 2016, leading up to the expansion in February through $135, and ultimately up to $155 per share.

- RIPE FOR BREAKOUT
Reviewing Boeing's weekly chart:
- Boeing also had an old high in 2015.
- The market consolidated, with price running down below the $120 level to clear out sell stops, before
institutional order flow began supporting down-closed candles.
- There were clear levels at $150 and an old high around $160.
- As we moved into the bullish seasonal tendency in February 2017, the market was sitting right at old
highs, perfectly ripe for a breakout.
- Institutions favor this setup and will buy aggressively if the company is fundamentally sound, as Boeing is.
- From $160 a share, Boeing ultimately moved up to $200 a share, demonstrating excellent price
movement that fits the strong weekly scenario. Apple and Boeing were very strong candidates.

- WEAKER MARKET STRUCTURE
Reviewing Disney's weekly chart:
- Despite meeting initial filtering, Disney exhibited a weaker market structure compared to Apple and
Boeing.
- Old highs from summer 2015 and November were around $120, indicating consistent resistance at that
level.
- Price had already blown through a short-term high and, while there were equal highs around $120, it was
far less likely to appreciate significantly.
- Disney would have required a rally of about 15% just to reach the old high from 2015, indicating it needed
significantly more movement to achieve a new breakout.
- Institutions are less aggressive in buying this type of stock because it is not technically poised, regardless
of fundamentals, until it reaches a clear technical level like $120.
- Incidentally, in early 2017, many analysts called for $120 and $125, which, as a contrarian sentiment
idea, proved to be the top, as Disney moved just slightly over $116 before trading softer.

- STRONG BREAKOUT
Reviewing Home Depot's weekly chart:
- Home Depot was very similar to Boeing and Apple in its strength.
- During the February 2017 bullish seasonal tendency, when the Dow made a lower low, Home Depot was
unwilling to follow.
- Being very close to its old high, institutions stepped in to buy, surging price through the old high for a nice
breakout, resulting in a $20 per share appreciation.

- AGGRESSIVE RUN FROM ORDER
BLOCK
Reviewing McDonald's weekly chart:
- At the time of bullishness in 2017, price was trading just inside a larger consolidation.
- Price had respected a bullish order block around $120, setting up for a low resistance liquidity run to
around $132 a share.
- Price eventually broke out a few months later with a very aggressive run above $132, trading as high as
$155 a share.
- McDonald's, coupled with Apple, Boeing, and Home Depot, represents a total of four very strong
candidates.

- FAVORABLE BUT LESS AGGRESSIVE
Reviewing Visa's weekly chart:
- Very similar to the other strong candidates, in February 2017, Visa was trading very close to its highs.
- Institutions surged price through, leading to an appreciation of about $10 a share.
- While Visa's share return appreciation was less aggressive than the other four (McDonald's, Home Depot,
Boeing, and Apple), it nonetheless met the criteria for higher moves on the weekly chart, and its structure
was sound. It was still a favorable selection from the ferreting-out process.
CONCLUSION:

section_title: THE WINNING RECIPE FOR STOCK SELECTION
content:
When going through the process of identifying opportunities for February to spring highs in the stock
market (applicable to Dow 30, NASDAQ 100, or S&P 500, though the latter requires more work for more
selections):
- Once you identify stocks with potential weekly breakouts (as stocks are predisposed to trade higher):
- Combine a fundamental basis for expecting higher prices in the stock market as a whole.
- Look for aligning seasonal tendencies for the market to go higher.
- Apply relative strength ideas, supported by SMT divergence between the indices (where one index fails
to post a lower low).
- Ensure the specific stock is showing an unwillingness to go lower.
- This comprehensive approach provides every component of a winning recipe for that stock to go higher.
While it doesn't guarantee being right every time, statistically and historically, these elements are
consistently present in winning stocks.
